canonRefs.label: Captain Sol Ledger
canonRefs.preview:
# Captain Sol Ledger

Sol commands the _Outstanding Balance_, a cargo ship famous for completing impossible runs and submitting every expense correctly. He claims to be the forgotten heir of a stellar dynasty, but three different dynasties make the same claim and all use the same promotional photograph.

Sol helps Tess steal the [[Stellar Plot Coupon]] from [[Orbital Cantina Nine]]. His instinct is to improvise; his secret gift is noticing when improvisation has quietly become a recurring pattern.

## Complication

The ship's navigation system believes it is Sol's sarcastic co-protagonist and refuses routes that lack emotional payoff.

canonRefs.label: Duchess Spoiler
canonRefs.preview:
# Duchess Spoiler

Duchess Spoiler enters every room knowing what will happen five minutes later and is furious that nobody appreciates the efficiency. She was raised by a prestigious order of masked antagonists who reveal family secrets during unsafe architectural conditions.

The Duchess serves [[Continuity Studios]] because the studio gives her advance access to every script. She admires Tess's refusal to perform destiny correctly and may help expose the [[Reboot Cycle]] if Tess proves that surprise has value beyond opening-week revenue.

## Tell

When Duchess Spoiler says, "This is not the part where I betray you," it is always technically true and strategically unhelpful.

canonRefs.label: Nana Null
canonRefs.preview:
# Nana Null

Nana Null is a retired prophecy clerk who learned to erase names from destiny without erasing the people attached to them. She keeps forbidden drafts in recipe boxes and can identify a corrupted timeline by taste.

Years ago, Null helped design the negotiation layer inside the [[Continuity Engine]]. When [[Continuity Studios]] demanded one authoritative timeline, she hid an emergency option in the break-room vending machine.

## Secret

The supposedly mundane exit ending is not an illusion. Null built a small continuity outside the genre economy where people can have lunch without foreshadowing.

canonRefs.label: Paladin Patch
canonRefs.preview:
# Paladin Patch

Paladin Patch is Cape City's licensed emergency retcon specialist. She can temporarily repair broken physics, contradictory backstories, and costume seams, provided the incident is filed before midnight.

Patch carries the [[Emergency Cape Permit]], an authorization powerful enough to make public infrastructure behave heroically for one scene. She mistrusts [[Continuity Studios]] because every sponsored rescue adds a merchandising clause to the victim's medical paperwork.

## Limitation

Her powers solve immediate continuity errors but cannot decide which version deserves to survive. That choice belongs to Tess.

canonRefs.label: Professor Beige the Terminal
canonRefs.preview:
# Professor Beige the Terminal

Professor Beige is the only wizard to survive seven final battles by scheduling each one during office hours. His robes are aggressively neutral, his hat has tenure, and his wand doubles as a laser pointer.

He teaches Applied Foreshadowing at the [[Hexford Academy Annex]], where students learn that raw magical talent is no substitute for a valid [[Provisional Wand License]]. Beige knows the [[Continuity Engine]] was never meant to preserve a single perfect canon; it was designed to negotiate among competing versions.

## Advice

"A prophecy is merely a spoiler with institutional funding."

canonRefs.label: Sprig-Nine
canonRefs.preview:
# Sprig-Nine

Sprig-Nine is a small moss-covered creature with antlers, static electricity, and a comprehensive understanding of collective bargaining. Trainers once classified Sprig as a starter companion; Sprig now identifies as a labor organizer who occasionally fits in a decorative carrying orb.

Sprig guides Tess through the [[Collectible Wilds]] and negotiates access to the [[Mutual Aid Badge]]. Its battle cry translates roughly to: "Friendship is not an unpaid internship."

## Ability

Sprig can evolve into whatever form a scene requires, but deliberately remains small because the travel allowance is better.

canonRefs.label: Tess Quill
canonRefs.preview:
# Tess Quill

Tess is a junior continuity auditor whose greatest ambition was to finish a fiscal quarter without discovering a prophecy. During a routine inspection of the [[Continuity Engine]], every destiny index in the building prints her employee number under **Chosen One, Temporary, Benefits Pending**.

She is observant, dryly funny, and dangerously competent at finding contradictions. Tess can restore the existing canon, release stories from centralized control, accept a lucrative villain contract, authorize a spectacular reboot, or discover that the universe has a perfectly ordinary emergency exit.

## Core conflict

Tess wants people to choose their own lives, but she also understands why a shared world needs enough structure to remain legible. Her strongest weapon is the [[Red Sta

canonRefs.label: The Executive Producer
canonRefs.preview:
# The Executive Producer

No surviving document records the Executive Producer's original name. They appear as a perfectly tailored silhouette surrounded by floating audience metrics and speak exclusively in confident revisions.

The Producer intends to trigger the [[Reboot Cycle]], compressing the Grand Continuity into a safer, younger, more merchandise-compatible timeline. They are not trying to destroy the world; they are trying to make it easier to pitch.

## Offer

Tess can become Vice President of Destiny if she stamps the reboot as canon. The salary is excellent, the dental plan covers fangs, and the contract has no final page.

canonRefs.label: Canon Debt
canonRefs.preview:
# Canon Debt

Every forced correction to shared history creates Canon Debt: memories that still exist emotionally after being declared factually impossible. Enough debt produces ghosts, contradictory flashbacks, and citizens who remember attending their own origin stories twice.

The Ministry records debt as technical error. [[Nana Null]] treats it as testimony from erased worlds.

canonRefs.label: Chosen One Allocation
canonRefs.preview:
# Chosen One Allocation

Destiny is a finite civic resource assigned by the [[Office of Prophecy Allocation]]. Applicants receive priority for tragic childhoods, symbolic birthmarks, and demonstrated availability during finales.

The system chooses Tess because her audit credentials give her access to the Engine, then invents a prophecy after the fact to make the assignment feel timeless.

canonRefs.label: Fourth-Wall Weather
canonRefs.preview:
# Fourth-Wall Weather

Fourth-Wall Weather is the measurable influence of audience expectation on local reality. Forecasts include exposition fog, montage winds, grimdark pressure systems, and a forty percent chance of cameo.

[[Duchess Spoiler]] senses incoming fronts before anyone else, which is why she always carries an umbrella to emotional climaxes.

canonRefs.label: Genre Bleed
canonRefs.preview:
# Genre Bleed

Genre Bleed occurs when the rules, tone, or budget of one narrative region leak into another. Minor cases produce talking swords in police procedurals. Severe cases cause a romantic misunderstanding to acquire orbital weapons.

The [[Continuity Engine]] manages ordinary bleed by negotiating local rules. The [[Present Convergence]] overwhelms that negotiation layer.

canonRefs.label: Narrative Gravity
canonRefs.preview:
# Narrative Gravity

Narrative Gravity pulls meaningful people and objects toward locations with unresolved stakes. It explains why strangers meet in taverns, villains choose visible towers, and every train eventually stops near the protagonist.

[[Finale Galleria]] exploits the phenomenon commercially by offering discounted rent to prophecies with three-act structures.

canonRefs.label: Reboot Cycle
canonRefs.preview:
# Reboot Cycle

A Reboot Cycle collapses divergent history into one simplified timeline. Familiar roles return with revised ages, cleaner costumes, and selective amnesia. The discarded versions persist as [[Canon Debt]].

Natural reboots once occurred after catastrophic continuity failure. [[Continuity Studios]] has learned to trigger them deliberately whenever audience research reports excessive lore.

canonRefs.label: Bureaucratodon
canonRefs.preview:
# Bureaucratodon

A Bureaucratodon is a broad, armored creature whose scales resemble filing drawers. It can reject an attack for incomplete documentation or return it to the sender with comments.

The specimen blocking the ranger office is not hostile; it is waiting for six months of unpaid habitat reimbursement. Resolving its grievance earns the [[Mutual Aid Badge]].

canonRefs.label: Continuity Slime
canonRefs.preview:
# Continuity Slime

Continuity Slimes absorb minor contradictions and adopt traits from every version. A slime exposed to three conflicting costume descriptions becomes plaid, armored, sleeveless, and deeply tired.

The Ministry treats them as maintenance equipment. Freeplot insists they are oral historians with unconventional methods.

canonRefs.label: Fussgriff
canonRefs.preview:
# Fussgriff

The Fussgriff resembles a pocket-sized griffin wearing the expression of an underappreciated stage actor. It repeats nearby dialogue with improved projection and refuses combat unless given top billing.

Fussgriffs nest in discarded title cards and can carry one small object or one enormous grudge.

canonRefs.label: Spoilerfinch
canonRefs.preview:
# Spoilerfinch

Spoilerfinches chirp the final sentence of any conversation they overhear. Most predictions are accurate, but the birds cannot distinguish a definitive ending from a sarcastic draft.

[[Duchess Spoiler]] keeps one as a companion and argues with it professionally.

canonRefs.label: The Cape Permit Crisis
canonRefs.preview:
# The Cape Permit Crisis

Seven simultaneous skyline beams caused rolling dramatic blackouts across Cape City. Local 404 negotiated a licensing framework that reduced collateral damage and guaranteed villains access to safer monologue platforms.

The [[Emergency Cape Permit]] was created as part of the settlement.

canonRefs.label: The First Merger
canonRefs.preview:
# The First Merger

Budgetary pressure combined seven narrative realities into the Grand Continuity. The operation saved infrastructure costs but placed three dark towers, two chosen villages, and one cheerful creature preserve on the same commuter line.

The [[Continuity Engine]] was built to negotiate the resulting contradictions.

canonRefs.label: The Great Prophecy Recall
canonRefs.preview:
# The Great Prophecy Recall

Thousands of prophecies were recalled after investigators discovered they all selected the same brooding teenager. [[Nana Null]] led the repair effort and quietly added an opt-out field to destiny records.

The opt-out field remains technically available but is printed in invisible ink.

canonRefs.label: The Pocket Beast Strike
canonRefs.preview:
# The Pocket Beast Strike

Creatures across the Wilds refused random encounters until trainers recognized rest days, consent, and shared merchandising revenue. The strike ended peacefully when Sprig-Nine evolved into a labor negotiator during live arbitration.

The resulting agreement created the [[Mutual Aid Badge]].

canonRefs.label: The Present Convergence
canonRefs.preview:
# The Present Convergence

The [[Continuity Engine]] begins collapsing all genre regions into a single market-tested timeline. Every prophecy assigns Tess Quill as temporary chosen one, and the Executive Producer schedules a reboot for midnight.

This is the historical event dramatized by [[The Last Acceptable Reboot]]. Its final canonical outcome is intentionally unresolved so the PathBranching endings can demonstrate alternate conclusions.

canonRefs.label: The Generic Fantasy Realm
canonRefs.preview:
# The Generic Fantasy Realm

Una demo de fantasía genérica para probar personajes, lugares, organizaciones, magia, quests y escenas usando la plantilla Worldbuilding por defecto.

canonRefs.label: Continuity Engine
canonRefs.preview:
# Continuity Engine

The Engine is a city-sized machine beneath [[Finale Galleria]]. It does not decide which story is true. Its original purpose was to let contradictory stories coexist while preserving enough shared facts for people to meet each other.

The Ministry gradually converted negotiation into enforcement. [[Continuity Studios]] then added a reboot control disguised as a user-experience improvement.

## Four-key interface

The Engine recognizes four genre credentials: the [[Stellar Plot Coupon]], [[Provisional Wand License]], [[Mutual Aid Badge]], and [[Emergency Cape Permit]]. Together they grant access to the final continuity console.

canonRefs.label: Emergency Cape Permit
canonRefs.preview:
# Emergency Cape Permit

The Permit authorizes one person to commandeer dramatic lighting, rooftop access, and a safe section of skyline during a declared continuity emergency.

Its reverse side lists prohibited actions, including anonymous property damage, unscheduled origin stories, and beams whose color has not been cleared with air traffic control.

canonRefs.label: Mutual Aid Badge
canonRefs.preview:
# Mutual Aid Badge

The Badge certifies that its holder solved a creature conflict without forced capture, elemental exploitation, or an inspirational speech delivered before listening.

It is also a key to the [[Continuity Engine]], whose original designers considered consent a foundational compatibility protocol.

canonRefs.label: Provisional Wand License
canonRefs.preview:
# Provisional Wand License

This laminated credential permits the holder to perform one plot-relevant spell under faculty supervision. It glows when used responsibly and emits a disappointed cough when waved theatrically.

[[Professor Beige]] awards it after Tess solves an exam by questioning the premise rather than choosing one of the four suspiciously faction-coded answers.

canonRefs.label: Red Stamp of Canon
canonRefs.preview:
# Red Stamp of Canon

The Stamp makes one declarative sentence true across the Grand Continuity. It cannot resolve ambiguity; it merely relocates the ambiguity into footnotes, spin-offs, and increasingly tense family dinners.

Tess is issued the Stamp when assigned to audit the [[Continuity Engine]]. Using it enables the Patched Canon ending, while refusing it strengthens Freeplot's argument that no person should own universal truth.

canonRefs.label: Stellar Plot Coupon
canonRefs.preview:
# Stellar Plot Coupon

A holographic voucher redeemable for one maneuver that ignores fuel, distance, and previously established ship capabilities. The fine print requires an emotional confession during acceleration.

The coupon is kept in [[Orbital Cantina Nine]] until Tess and [[Captain Sol Ledger]] prove their heist is advancing character rather than merely filling runtime.

canonRefs.label: Cape City Permit Hall
canonRefs.preview:
# Cape City Permit Hall

Permit Hall is both city hall and an emergency-response headquarters. Its roof opens for dramatic departures, while its basement contains the Department of Secret Identities and a very public lost-and-found.

Tess must earn the [[Emergency Cape Permit]] by resolving a crisis without causing a third-act skyline beam. [[Paladin Patch]] considers this the hardest test in modern heroism.

canonRefs.label: Collectible Wilds
canonRefs.preview:
# Collectible Wilds

The Wilds consist of grass routes, elemental caves, improbably specialized habitats, and health centers placed at legally mandated intervals. Creature encounters are voluntary under the [[Pocket Colossi Union]] agreement.

Visitors seeking the [[Mutual Aid Badge]] must help settle a dispute between a [[Bureaucratodon]] and the ranger office rather than defeat a conveniently ranked gym leader.

canonRefs.label: Finale Galleria
canonRefs.preview:
# Finale Galleria

Finale Galleria is a shopping complex built around the exposed chamber of the [[Continuity Engine]]. Every corridor converges on the central atrium because [[Narrative Gravity]] has a retail partnership.

The mall contains abandoned sets from previous timelines, a food court that serves symbolic meals, and the flagship offices of [[Continuity Studios]]. During the [[Present Convergence]], its skylight becomes the mandatory beam through which the reboot signal will fire.

canonRefs.label: Hexford Academy Annex
canonRefs.preview:
# Hexford Academy Annex

Hexford is an ancient school of magic operating from a modern office park because the original castle failed accessibility inspection. The moving staircases were replaced with elevators that still enjoy riddles.

Students sort themselves by preferred citation style. Professor Beige guards the [[Provisional Wand License]] in the Department of Applied Foreshadowing, where every exam question has already been foretold but the grading rubric remains mysterious.

canonRefs.label: Meridian Junction
canonRefs.preview:
# Meridian Junction

Meridian Junction is a vertical city built around the rail lines of [[Narrative Gravity]]. Trains arrive from enchanted kingdoms, orbital republics, creature preserves, wasteland suburbs, and Cape City, often on the same platform.

The skyline changes with audience expectations. Towers become castles during fantasy weather, starships during space-opera weather, and suspiciously affordable apartments during romantic-comedy fronts.

## Districts

- The Ministry Stack, containing [[Office of Prophecy Allocation]].
- Union Platform, where the [[Freeplot Cooperative]] distributes unauthorized maps.
- The Galleria Line, terminating at [[Finale Galleria]].

canonRefs.label: Office of Prophecy Allocation
canonRefs.preview:
# Office of Prophecy Allocation

The Office assigns destinies, processes chosen-one appeals, and maintains a waiting list for dark lords seeking age-appropriate nemeses. Its lobby number display has shown **NOW SERVING: 3** for six centuries.

Tess begins [[The Last Acceptable Reboot]] here after every prophecy printer produces her employee number. A mislabeled staff corridor hides the first clue to Nana Null's ordinary emergency exit.

canonRefs.label: Orbital Cantina Nine
canonRefs.preview:
# Orbital Cantina Nine

Every booth in Cantina Nine hosts a different clandestine meeting. The house band knows only one suspicious melody but plays it in seventeen alien time signatures.

The [[Stellar Plot Coupon]] is stored in the manager's safe and may be redeemed for one impossible space maneuver. [[Captain Sol Ledger]] has a plan involving forged invoices, a ceremonial moon, and no fewer than two ventilation shafts.

canonRefs.label: Continuity Studios
canonRefs.preview:
# Continuity Studios

Continuity Studios finances heroic infrastructure in exchange for adaptation rights, naming rights, and the right to replace any confusing citizen with a younger interpretation. Its research division turned the [[Reboot Cycle]] from a cosmic accident into a quarterly strategy.

The Studio's pitch is seductive: one clean timeline, universal accessibility, spectacular visuals, and no homework. The cost is every inconvenient memory that made the world specific.

## Story route

Cooperating with the Studio raises spectacle and unlocks **Prestige Reboot** or **Executive Promotion**.

properties.ideology: Every universe deserves a clear brand promise and a recurring revenue model.

frontmatter.name: Continuity Studios
frontmatter.ideology: Every universe deserves a clear brand promise and a recurring revenue model.

canonRefs.label: Freeplot Cooperative
canonRefs.preview:
# Freeplot Cooperative

Freeplot is a decentralized network of side characters, cancelled villains, background citizens, and creatures who survived their original narrative functions. They repair abandoned subplots and publish continuity patches under permissive licenses.

The Cooperative wants to open the [[Continuity Engine]] so every community can maintain its own branch. Their plan is generous but risks turning shared history into incompatible local forks.

## Story route

Working with Freeplot builds solidarity and unlocks the **Stories in Common** ending.

properties.ideology: Stories should be maintained by their participants, not owned by a central authority.

frontmatter.name: Freeplot Cooperative
frontmatter.ideology: Stories should be maintained by their participants, not owned by a central authority.

canonRefs.label: Licensed Wizard Faculty
canonRefs.preview:
# Licensed Wizard Faculty

The Faculty accredits spell programs, regulates dramatic robes, and investigates plagiarism between ancient prophecies. Its members defend academic freedom while requiring seventeen signatures to summon a familiar.

The [[Provisional Wand License]] is their contribution to the four credentials needed to operate the [[Continuity Engine]].

properties.ideology: Magic without peer review is merely special effects.

frontmatter.name: Licensed Wizard Faculty
frontmatter.ideology: Magic without peer review is merely special effects.

canonRefs.label: Ministry of Narrative Compliance
canonRefs.preview:
# Ministry of Narrative Compliance

The Ministry maintains the official timeline, registers chosen ones, and issues continuity citations to paradoxes parked in loading zones. It is slow, sincere, and terrified that one unregulated side quest could collapse public transportation.

Most employees want stability rather than power. The institution's failure is believing that a single approved story can represent everyone who lives inside it.

## Story route

Supporting the Ministry raises compliance and unlocks the **Patched Canon** ending, provided Tess gathers all four genre credentials.

properties.ideology: A shared canon requires rules, forms, and properly licensed destinies.

frontmatter.name: Ministry of Narrative Compliance
frontmatter.ideology: A shared canon requires rules, forms, and properly licensed destinies.

canonRefs.label: Municipal Heroes Local 404
canonRefs.preview:
# Municipal Heroes Local 404

Local 404 organizes caped responders, dispatchers, sidekicks, bridge engineers, and citizens who point toward danger. Members receive health coverage, identity protection, and one dramatic rooftop conversation per quarter.

The Local controls the [[Emergency Cape Permit]] and refuses to issue it for battles whose primary purpose is improving a villain's skyline silhouette.

properties.ideology: Heroism is public service, not private branding.

frontmatter.name: Municipal Heroes Local 404
frontmatter.ideology: Heroism is public service, not private branding.

canonRefs.label: Pocket Colossi Union
canonRefs.preview:
# Pocket Colossi Union

The Union represents creatures previously expected to fight strangers, live in tiny containers, and communicate through marketable syllables. Its contract recognizes evolution as a personal choice and friendship as a relationship rather than a combat bonus.

The Union awards the [[Mutual Aid Badge]] to travelers who resolve conflict without forced capture.

properties.ideology: Companionship requires consent, rest periods, and profit participation.

frontmatter.name: Pocket Colossi Union
frontmatter.ideology: Companionship requires consent, rest periods, and profit participation.

canonRefs.label: Cape Permit Test
canonRefs.preview:
# Cape Permit Test

[[Paladin Patch]] asks Tess to stop an unauthorized skyline beam without punching its operator through a landmark. The solution requires evacuation, labor coordination, and reading the machine's cancellation policy.

Local 404 awards the [[Emergency Cape Permit]].

canonRefs.label: The Continuity Choice
canonRefs.preview:
# The Continuity Choice

At the [[Continuity Engine]] console, Tess selects what kind of ending the world deserves. Route variables and earlier discoveries unlock different responses, but the Eternal Sequel remains available whenever a decisive conclusion feels too responsible.

The secret Ordinary Lunch ending appears only if Tess noticed the break-room exit during [[Prophecy Overflow]].

canonRefs.label: Creature Arbitration
canonRefs.preview:
# Creature Arbitration

A [[Bureaucratodon]] blocks the ranger office over unpaid habitat reimbursement. Tess and [[Sprig-Nine]] can listen, mediate, or attempt a traditional battle that immediately fails an ethics review.

A fair settlement earns the [[Mutual Aid Badge]].

canonRefs.label: Finale Galleria Infiltration
canonRefs.preview:
# Finale Galleria Infiltration

With four credentials, Tess enters [[Finale Galleria]]. [[Duchess Spoiler]] reveals that the Executive Producer has already filmed several endings and offers Tess the chance to improvise one they cannot predict.

canonRefs.label: Orbital Coupon Heist
canonRefs.preview:
# Orbital Coupon Heist

[[Captain Sol Ledger]] and Tess infiltrate [[Orbital Cantina Nine]] to recover the [[Stellar Plot Coupon]]. The plan can succeed through forged invoices, impossible piloting, or an honest conversation with the manager about unpaid narrative labor.

canonRefs.label: Prophecy Overflow
canonRefs.preview:
# Prophecy Overflow

Every printer in the [[Office of Prophecy Allocation]] produces Tess's employee number. She follows a corridor labeled **NOT A SECRET PASSAGE**, notices the break-room emergency exit, and reaches the audit chamber where [[Nana Null]] explains the crisis.

## Choice pressure

Tess may accept the [[Red Stamp of Canon]], question the Ministry's authority, or ask who profits from the reboot.

canonRefs.label: Route Selection
canonRefs.preview:
# Route Selection

Three representatives present incompatible plans in the same transit lounge. The Ministry wants restoration, Freeplot wants distributed stewardship, and the Studio wants a spectacular reboot.

The routes reconverge because all three need the same four credentials, but each alliance changes Tess's available final arguments.

canonRefs.label: Wand License Exam
canonRefs.preview:
# Wand License Exam

At [[Hexford Academy Annex]], [[Professor Beige]] administers a foretold exam. Tess earns the [[Provisional Wand License]] by identifying the hidden fifth answer: reject the assumption that power proves moral authority.

canonRefs.label: The Last Acceptable Reboot
canonRefs.preview:
# The Last Acceptable Reboot

When every prophecy printer names junior auditor [[Tess Quill]] as the temporary chosen one, she must inspect the failing [[Continuity Engine]] before [[The Executive Producer]] compresses reality into a cleaner, younger reboot.

Tess chooses whether to cooperate with the [[Ministry of Narrative Compliance]], the [[Freeplot Cooperative]], or [[Continuity Studios]]. With help from [[Captain Sol Ledger]], [[Professor Beige]], [[Sprig-Nine]], and [[Paladin Patch]], she gathers four genre credentials and reaches the final console beneath [[Finale Galleria]].

## Themes

- Shared canon versus personal agency.
- Affectionate parody of genre infrastructure.
- The difference between maintaining continuity and owning it.
- Whether a satisfying ending must be definitive

canonRefs.preview:

Heroes, rivals, mentors, villains, and suspiciously articulate companions who can appear in WorldNotion canon or PathBranching events.

canonRefs.label: README
canonRefs.preview:
# aux-demworld — The Grand Continuity

An original, synthetic demonstration universe for Everend Forge. It shows how the same vault can be explored in WorldNotion, authored as a branching narrative in PathBranching, and published as a read-only site with Compendium.

The setting parodies broad genre conventions—space dynasties, wizard schools, collectible creatures, municipal superheroes, chosen-one prophecies, and corporate reboots—without using characters, names, settings, or lore from existing franchises.

## What is included

- A connected canon of characters, locations, organizations, items, creatures, concepts, and historical events.
- A custom Everend taxonomy and reusable entity templates.
- A modular PathBranching v0.2 story named **The Last Acceptable Reboot**.
- Four major route
canonRefs.identityWarning: This note has no WorldNotion frontmatter/id.

canonRefs.label: Stories
canonRefs.preview:
# Stories

WorldNotion story and scene planning entities. Runtime branching data lives in `.everend/.pathbranching/`.
canonRefs.identityWarning: This note is missing id, type, or name in frontmatter.

canonRefs.label: aux demworld
canonRefs.preview:
# The Grand Continuity

The Grand Continuity is what happened when several perfectly serviceable genres were merged by a cost-saving initiative and nobody preserved the original backups. Star dynasties, spell academies, collectible creatures, caped municipal responders, post-apocalyptic homeowner associations, and prophecy offices now share one reality held together by the overworked [[Continuity Engine]].

At the center lies [[Meridian Junction]], where narrative gravity pulls every quest toward the same transit hub, food court, and suspiciously final shopping complex. The official history is maintained by the [[Ministry of Narrative Compliance]], challenged by the [[Freeplot Cooperative]], and monetized by [[Continuity Studios]].

The tone is adventurous, affectionate, and absurd. The wo
canonRefs.identityWarning: This note is missing id, type, or name in frontmatter.

canonRefs.label: README
canonRefs.preview: # YAML `yaml` is a definitive library for [YAML](https://yaml.org/), the human friendly data serialization standard. This library: - Supports both YAML 1.1 and YAML 1.2 and all common data schemas, - Passes all of the [yaml-test-suite](https://github.com/yaml/yaml-test-suite) tests, - Can accept any string as input without throwing, parsing as much YAML out of it as it can, and - Supports parsing, modifying, and writing YAML comments and blank lines. The library is released under the ISC open source license, and the code is [available on GitHub](https://github.com/eemeli/yaml/). It has no external dependencies and runs on Node.js as well as modern browsers.
canonRefs.identityWarning: This note has no WorldNotion frontmatter/id.

blocks.content: I would like to appeal this destiny on the grounds that I have a meeting at ten.
A healthy continuity is a conversation, dear. A dead one is a filing cabinet with excellent branding.
Magic is the easy part. The difficult part is deciding who must live with it afterward.
They filmed six endings. Let us make the seventh thing they fear: an unscheduled decision.
You can save every part of the world that tests well. I can even put your name above the title.
